title: Personalizing Chronic Pain Treatment Using Person-Specific Networks: A Single-Case Experimental Design Study
authors: Saskia Scholten, Verena Eunike Hofmann, Felicitas Kininger, Julia Glombiewski
description:
Objective: Complex disorders such as chronic pain require individualized clinical decision-making, yet existing treatment frameworks provide limited guidance at the individual level. This proof-of-concept study evaluated the feasibility and acceptability of using person-specific networks to guide treatment target selection and match evidence-based intervention modules.
Method: Twelve patients with chronic pain and comorbidities (MAge = 60.25, 58% identified as female) and nine therapists participated. Qualitative interviews assessing feasibility and acceptability were conducted following treatment. Treatment effects were evaluated using an ABCD Single-Case Experimental Design with randomized baselines. Quantitative outcomes included compliance and individual- and group-level analyses of disability measures. 
Results: Mean observations per participant were 298; ≥80% compliance was achieved in seven participants across phases. Qualitative analyses highlighted participant-centered assessment design for feasibility and acceptability. Self-monitoring and structured data-informed reflection were identified as central mechanisms for increasing awareness of everyday processes and supporting therapeutic reflection. Exploratory individual-level analyses showed mixed treatment effects, whereas multilevel analysis indicated an average decrease in disability scores from baseline to intervention phase (β₁ = -0.45, p = .03). 
Conclusions: The clinical value of data-informed personalization may not lie in algorithmic precision alone, but rather in its ability to facilitate patient-centered, reflective clinical reasoning in complex cases.

title: The Interplay Between Cognitive-Behavioural and -Interpersonal Factors in Disordered Eating: A Network Analysis
authors: Chantal P Delaquis, Yasmin Brown, Dean Spirou, Jayanthi (Jay) Raman, Sylvie Berthoz
description:
Eating disorders (ED) involve distressing cognitions related to weight, shape and eating, as well as
disordered eating behaviours. The transdiagnostic cognitive-behavioral and cognitive-interpersonal
models are leading frameworks used to understand ED pathology. Although these two models highlight
complementary maintenance mechanisms, they have not yet been evaluated simultaneously. The current
study aims to integrate the core cognitive-behavioral and cognitive-interpersonal dimensions using
network analysis to examine their interrelationships across the spectrum of ED severity.
Method
A partial correlation network was estimated in a sample of Australian community/students
reporting ED symptoms (n = 493; n = 271 were above the Eating Disorder Examination Questionnaire Short
(EDE-QS) clinical cutoff; n = 108 reported a lifetime ED diagnosis). We evaluated topological overlap, node
predictability, and expected influence (EI) and bridge EI (BEI). Bootstrapped stability and centrality
difference tests were conducted.
Results
The network had excellent stability (all CS = 0.75). Central nodes were Concern Over Mistakes (EI
= 1.48), Impulse/NonGoals (EI = 1.41) and Overvaluation of Weight and Shape (EI = 1.35). Concern Over
Mistakes was the most significant bridge node (BEI = 0.86). One of the strongest edges in the network was
between Overvaluation of Weight and Shape and Weight and Shape Rigidity (edge = 0.45).
Conclusions
Our results support the robust and significant role of Concern Over Mistakes as a central ED
symptom. The high bridge centrality of Concern Over Mistakes indicates that it may serve as a pathway
connecting symptom clusters. Rigid beliefs about weight and shape may maintain disordered eating
symptoms.

title: "It's a balancing act": Autistic fathers navigating parenting in everyday life
authors: Chris Edwards, Abigail Love, Ru Ying Cai, Kees-jan Archbald, Dean Dickson, Sean Hynes, Abel Trindade, Sarah Edwards, Bronwyn Leigh, Vicki Gibbs
description:
Background
Autistic parents are increasingly recognized as an important source of insight into Autistic adulthood and family life, yet the perspectives of Autistic people who identify as fathers remain almost entirely absent from research. Fathers have distinct experiences, and the absence of their stories and perspectives limits understanding of Autistic parenting more broadly. This study addressed this gap by exploring how Autistic fathers understand and experience parenting across the lifespan.
Methods
We co-produced the study with four Autistic fathers who contributed to design, interview development, and analysis. Nineteen Autistic fathers took part in semi-structured interviews, either spoken or written, depending on their preference. We analyzed the data using reflexive thematic analysis informed by constructionist and participatory research principles.
Results
Our analysis generated five themes: (1) Finding joy and meaning in everyday moments, which captured the emotional rewards of fatherhood; (2) Becoming myself through fatherhood, describing how parenting strengthened self-understanding and Autistic identity; (3) Walking the tightrope of family life, reflecting the effort of balancing sensory, emotional, and practical demands; (4) Parenting as relational work, showing how fathers negotiated understanding and communication within family relationships and the tension that arose when they felt misunderstood; and (5) Parenting in systems stacked against us, highlighting the barriers encountered in services and wider society.
Conclusion
Autistic fathers’ accounts deepen understanding of Autistic adulthood and family life. Their experiences show that fatherhood can strengthen identity and connection while exposing the limits of systems that rarely recognize Autistic needs. These fathers’ insights call for change in how research, policy, and services engage with Autistic people and their families.

title: Too Little, Too Much: Evidence for Quadratic Patterns in Emotion Regulation Strategy Use in Daily Life
authors: Dominique Maciejewski, Yasemin Erbas, Tak Tsun Lo, Egon Dejonckheere, Merlijn Olthof, Andrea Bunge, Eeske van Roekel
description: Emotion characteristics, like emotion intensity and perceived controllability, shape how individuals regulate their emotions. While the link between emotion characteristics and emotion regulation strategies seems clear for some strategies, findings are mixed for others. This study proposes that individuals are most likely to engage in emotion regulation at average levels of emotion intensity and controllability, and less likely at low or high levels (i.e., when emotions are either too weak to require regulation or too overwhelming to regulate). To test this hypothesis, we used data from a 60-day diary study in 79 adults (mean age = 22.29, SD = 6.08, 84% female), who every evening reported on emotion characteristics (emotion intensity and controllability) and strategy use (distraction, rumination, reappraisal, expression, suppression, relaxation). In addition to single strategies, we also examined emotion regulation strategy switching (i.e., the extent to which individuals varied their strategy composition). We replicate some linear findings, including that expression, relaxation, and rumination were used on days with more intense and uncontrollable emotions (for expression only). Importantly, we provide novel evidence for quadratic relations, where suppression and reappraisal were used on days where emotions were of average intensity and controllability, and less on days where emotions were less or more intense and controllable than usual. Moreover, individuals were more likely to switch to strategies they typically do not use on days where emotions were less or more controllable than usual. These findings highlight the value of moving beyond linear relations when studying emotion regulation dynamics in daily life.

title: Gaming Disorder:  A qualitative meta-synthesis of related case studies
authors: Tiina Auranen, Matus Adamkovic, Marcel Martončik, Solip Park, Joona Ville-Valtteri Kauraoja, Yaewon Jin, Ville Malinen, Mikhail Fiadotau, Sabina Hnatová, Anna Urbancová, Lenka Vargová, Jana Papcunová, Artem Buts, Sunny Choi, Veli-Matti Karhulahti
description: Several meta-analyses and reviews have been published on gaming-related health problems, mainly in the context of addictive behaviours and gaming disorder in particular. However, none of those reviews have been qualitative or focused on individual cases. The present meta-synthesis provides such evidence from 88 published case studies with 133 individual cases. Surprisingly, almost no case study evidence was found from high-prevalence countries like China and South Korea. Using deductive coding, the study identifies and synthesises a total of 845 problems and 35 comorbidities along with reasons for seeking help—most of treatment-seeking continues to happen due to social compliance. Further, we applied thematic synthesis to qualitatively organise the data into a network, represented by connected areas where a spectrum of vulnerabilities sets the individual on a high-stress life path; self-medication through gaming becomes both the cure and the problem; and prolonged gaming or critical life events lead to treatment or even hospitalization. After sensitivity analyses, primary findings generally hold throughout levels of informativeness across studies. We find many of the reported problems and their contexts aligning with coping theories of gaming disorder. Next to the above thematic areas, which characterises a large part of the published studies, we highlight the great variety of content across cases: most studies did not address, diagnose, or mention gaming disorder formally at all, but rather represented instances of life where gaming was related to health problems in other ways.

title: Normalization of anticipatory neural activity in PTSD after EMDR psychotherapy
authors: Raffaele Costanzo, Biancamaria Di Bello, Maria Gabriela Bevacqua, Francesco Di Russo, Francesca Strappini, Valentina Sulpizio, Sabrina Pitzalis
description:
Post traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is associated with persistent alterations in cognitive control and decision making, yet the neurophysiological mechanisms underlying psychotherapy related improvement remain incompletely understood. In particular, little is known about how evidence based treatments modulate proactive neural processes supporting anticipation and preparatory control. The present study investigated the clinical, behavioral, and neurophysiological effects of Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) psychotherapy in individuals with PTSD, with a specific focus on pre stimulus neural activity.
Eighteen patients with PTSD and twenty four age  and sex matched healthy controls performed a discriminative response task during electroencephalographic EEG recording at baseline (T0) and follow up (T1). Patients underwent EMDR psychotherapy, whereas controls were reassessed after a comparable time interval. Clinical symptoms, behavioral performance, and pre stimulus event related potential (ERP) components indexing anticipatory motor preparation and proactive cognitive control - namely the Bereitschaftspotential (BP) and the prefrontal Negativity (pN) - were analyzed.
At baseline, patients with PTSD showed greater symptom severity, slower and more variable behavioral responses, higher error rates, and reduced BP and pN amplitudes relative to controls. Following EMDR psychotherapy, patients exhibited significant reductions in anxiety, depressive, and dissociative symptoms, accompanied by faster, more stable, and more accurate task performance. Critically, BP and pN amplitudes increased after treatment, such that post treatment values no longer differed from those of healthy controls. No significant changes were observed in the control group across sessions.
Overall, these findings suggest that EMDR psychotherapy improves clinical symptoms, behavioral functioning, and anticipatory neural processes involved in cognitive control and response preparation in PTSD.

title: Sensitive periods for childhood adversity and their impact on later life outcomes: A narrative review
authors: Dr Chris Patrick Pflanz, John Gallacher, Sarah Bauermeister
description:
Rationale. Childhood adversity can impair mental and physical health later in life, and these deleterious effects may depend on sensitive periods for child abuse during which children are particularly vulnerable. The purpose of this narrative review is to scope previous research on sensitive periods of child adversity that are linked with negative later life outcomes.
Methods. Only peer-reviewed original research articles were included in the review. Relevant key information was extracted from the articles and themes were identified as repeated patterns of meaning-making that evolve around a similar interpretation of the research findings.
Findings. From the scoped literature on sensitive periods for childhood adversity, three themes were identified: (1) The earlier the childhood adversity in life the worse, (2) There are two sensitive periods that correspond to periods of change in development: early childhood and early adolescence. (3) Sensitive periods for child adversity are domain-specific and depend on the aspect of impaired psychosocial functioning.
Conclusions. Despite evidence for sensitive periods for child adversity, it is too early to describe normative, sensitive periods because previous findings were largely study-specific. More research is needed to investigate the impact of child abuse on various domains of psychosocial functioning later in life. More prospective, and longitudinal research is also needed because current findings are subject to retrospective memory bias. Overall, when child abuse occurred during the lifespan may be important information in optimising and personalising treatment options.

title: Indigenous Perspectives of Trauma-Sensitive Yoga Using Community-Based and Consensual Qualitative Frameworks
authors: Kelsey Madison Dietrich, Linda Zheng, Ashley Baumann, Jill Kessler, Lyndsey Ducheneaux, Viann N Nguyen-Feng
description:
Objective: Indigenous communities endure intergenerational and ongoing traumas. Trauma Center Trauma-Sensitive Yoga (TCTSY) is an empirically-supported yoga-based intervention for complex trauma. To become a TCTSY-Facilitator, candidates complete a 20-hour training followed by a 300-hour certification program. This community-based research documented the first Indigenous TCTSY-Facilitator Training Cohort’s perspectives on TCTSY’s acceptability, feasibility, and utility. 
Method: Protocols were developed with community partners, and researchers engaged in reflexivity practices and Indigenous cultural learnings. Twenty interviews were conducted with 15 adult interviewees: 14 program participants (13 women) who completed the 20-hour training and 1 program mentor. From the 14 program participants (11 Great Plains Tribal Nations, with 4 Tribal citizens and 7 descendents), 10 enrolled in the 300-hour training, and 3 completed the full certification. Interviews were analyzed using the consensual qualitative research method. 
Results: Most participants reported the training positively impacted their approach to trauma care and some aspects (e.g., body-based modality) aligned with current healing approaches. Common training takeaways included learning about the effects of trauma and the value of invitational language. Participants endorsed intentions to integrate TCTSY for personal and community healing; examples of integration included smudging/burning of medicines (e.g., sage), land-based practice for connection to nature, and intentional movement within ceremony space. Program improvements included additional emphasis on yoga history/philosophy, collaborations with Elders, and acknowledgment of current community trauma. 
Conclusion: A community-based research framework provided opportunities to explore the feasibility, acceptability, and utility of TCTSY for Indigenous communities in a culturally-relevant and trauma-informed way.
